     1|# Examples: Real-World Workflow
     2|
     3|## Example 1: Teresa Torres' Continuous Discovery Article
     4|
     5|### Initial Request
     6|
     7|"I'm writing about continuous discovery for product managers. It's a topic I care deeply about but it feels abstract to readers. Help me create an outline that lands with specific examples."
     8|
     9|### Clarifying Questions (from me)
    10|
    11|- Who's the target audience? (Product managers mid-career)
    12|- What's the unique angle? (Moving from annual roadmaps to continuous learning)
    13|- What's the goal? (Help PMs see continuous discovery as essential practice)
    14|- Existing research? (Yes, customer interviews and my own data from 5 companies)
    15|- Desired length? (~2000 words)
    16|- Writing style? (Direct, practical, conversational with authority)
    17|
    18|### Outline Created Together
    19|
    20|```markdown
    21|# Outline: Continuous Discovery as Core PM Practice
    22|
    23|## Hook
    24|
    25|- Opening: "I watched a PM ignore 15 customer interviews because they were already locked into a roadmap"
    26|- Why reader should care: This is happening in your org right now
    27|
    28|## The Problem (Section 1)
    29|
    30|- Annual planning creates lock-in
    31|- Customer reality changes monthly
    32|- PMs make decisions with stale data
    33|- Examples: [Teresa's research from 5 companies]
    34|
    35|## What Continuous Discovery Actually Is (Section 2)
    36|
    37|- Definition: Structured learning that informs every decision
    38|- NOT just customer interviews
    39|- System for rapid insight → action
    40|- Framework: [Teresa's specific approach]
    41|
    42|## How to Start (Section 3)
    43|
    44|- First discovery session (concrete steps)
    45|- Common mistakes (what NOT to do)
    46|- Quick wins: [Specific examples]
    47|
    48|## Measuring Impact (Section 4)
    49|
    50|- Metrics that show continuous discovery is working
    51|- How to know you're doing it right
    52|- Company cases: [Data from her 5 companies]
    53|
    54|## Conclusion
    55|
    56|- This isn't optional anymore
    57|- Call to action: "Start with just one team"
    58|- Final thought: Why this matters
    59|
    60|## Research Needed
    61|
    62|- [ ] Format customer interview templates
    63|- [ ] Compile metrics from 5 companies
    64|- [ ] Find 2-3 external studies on discovery effectiveness
    65|```
    66|
    67|### After Outline Approval
    68|
    69|Teresa wrote Section 1 (The Problem) → I provided feedback focusing on clarity and examples → She revised → Moved to Section 2.
    70|
    71|**Feedback on Section 1:**
    72|
    73|```
    74|What Works Well ✓
    75|- Opening story is gripping (immediate tension)
    76|- Three examples of lock-in feel specific, not generic
    77|- Your voice comes through (direct, slightly frustrated tone)
    78|
    79|Suggestions:
    80|- The stat "15 customer interviews ignored" — do you have source for how common this is?
    81|  Could add credibility if cited. (Or remove if you want to keep it anecdotal)
    82|- Transition to "Here's why this matters" feels abrupt. Consider:
    83|  "This happens because [system reason]. Here's why it matters..."
    84|
    85|Your choice: Keep it punchy or add system context?
    86|
    87|Specific Edit:
    88|Original: "This is happening in your org right now"
    89|Suggested: "This is happening in your org right now, even if you don't see it"
    90|Why: Slightly more compelling accusation; makes it personal
    91|
    92|Ready to move to Section 2?
    93|```
    94|
    95|### Final Result
    96|
    97|2000-word article that reads like Teresa, sounds authoritative, has solid customer data backing every claim, and lands with her target audience (product managers who already respect her thinking).
    98|
    99|---
   100|
   101|## Example 2: Research-Heavy SaaS Technical Article
   102|
   103|### Initial Request
   104|
   105|"I need to write about AI's impact on product management tools. Very research-heavy. I have strong opinions but need data to back them up. Where do I start?"
   106|
   107|### Research Phase
   108|
   109|**Me:** "Let's identify what claims you want to make, then find sources for each."
   110|
   111|**Writer's Key Claims:**
   112|
   113|1. "AI will automate 40% of routine PM work"
   114|2. "Adoption is increasing but many PMs are skeptical"
   115|3. "The best use cases are [X, Y, Z]"
   116|
   117|### Research Compiled
   118|
   119|```markdown
   120|## Claim 1: AI Will Automate 40% of PM Work
   121|
   122|**Sources Found:**
   123|
   124|- Gartner: 45% of PM routine tasks automatable by 2025 [¹]
   125|- McKinsey: "AI and the Future of Product Development" (2024) [²]
   126|- Harvard Business Review: "When AI Augments PM Judgment" (2023) [³]
   127|
   128|**Writer's Choice:** Use Gartner stat (most authoritative); cite McKinsey for deeper insight
   129|
   130|---
   131|
   132|## Claim 2: Adoption Growing But Skepticism Remains
   133|
   134|**Sources Found:**
   135|
   136|- Stack Overflow 2024 Survey: 62% of PMs use AI tools; 31% highly skeptical [⁴]
   137|- Forrester: "Product Manager Adoption of AI" Q4 2024 [⁵]
   138|
   139|**Writer's Choice:** Use Stack Overflow (credible developer survey); link to Forrester for nuance
   140|
   141|---
   142|
   143|## Claim 3: Best Use Cases
   144|
   145|**Companies Using AI Effectively:**
   146|
   147|- Airbnb: AI for user research synthesis → 60% time savings [⁶]
   148|- Stripe: Competitive intelligence automation [⁷]
   149|- Figma: Design trend analysis [⁸]
   150|
   151|**Writer's Choice:** Use Airbnb + Stripe; research Figma independently to verify
   152|
   153|---
   154|
   155|[Full citation details below]
   156|```
   157|
   158|### Writer's Approval
   159|
   160|"Great, this gives me confidence. I'm going to use the Gartner stat but also explain why the McKinsey perspective matters for context."
   161|
   162|### Result
   163|
   164|Research-heavy article that's credible because sources are verified, properly attributed, and chosen strategically (not exhaustively).
   165|
   166|---
   167|
   168|## Example 3: Hook Improvement Iterations
   169|
   170|### Original Hook (Writer's First Draft)
   171|
   172|> "Artificial intelligence is changing product management. In this article, I explore how product managers can adapt to this shift."
   173|
   174|### My Analysis & Alternatives
   175|
   176|```
   177|Current Hook Analysis:
   178|- What works: You've identified an important topic
   179|- What could be stronger: It's generic (could apply to any trending topic)
   180|- Emotional impact: Low; reader has no reason to keep reading
   181|
   182|Three Alternative Approaches:
   183|
   184|Option 1 (Data-Driven):
   185|"I just reviewed AI tools used by product managers at 50 companies. 80%
   186|were solving the wrong problem. Here's how to actually use AI effectively."
   187|
   188|Why it works: Surprising stat (80% wrong) + promise of useful knowledge
   189|
   190|---
   191|
   192|Option 2 (Story-Based):
   193|"Last month, I watched a PM spend three days analyzing user feedback.
   194|A bot did the same analysis in 20 minutes. But the bot missed the insight that changed the roadmap."
   195|
   196|Why it works: Concrete story shows AI's limitation + value of human judgment
   197|
   198|---
   199|
   200|Option 3 (Question-Based):
   201|"How many hours is your team spending on work that AI could do in minutes?
   202|And more importantly: on what are you NOT spending time because of it?"
   203|
   204|Why it works: Makes reader think about opportunity cost, not just automation
   205|```
   206|
   207|### Writer's Choice
   208|
   209|"I like option 2 (story) because it matches my voice, but the third sentence needs work. Let me revise: 'But the bot missed the insight that convinced the CEO to fund our moonshot.'"
   210|
   211|**My Response:** "Better. Specific outcome matters. Ready to draft section 1?"
   212|
   213|### Result
   214|
   215|Hook that captures attention _and_ sounds like the writer (authentic voice + compelling).
   216|
   217|---
   218|
   219|## Example 4: Section Feedback in Action
   220|
   221|### Writer's Draft: "How to Structure Your Discovery Process"
   222|
   223|> "Product managers often struggle with discovery. Many teams don't have a systematic approach. This leads to missed opportunities and wasted time. A good discovery process should include interviews, surveys, and data analysis. You should do this monthly. You should also document what you learn."
   224|
   225|### Feedback Provided
   226|
   227|```
   228|What Works Well ✓
   229|- You're identifying a real problem that readers face
   230|- Clear structure (problem → solution framework)
   231|
   232|Suggestions for Improvement:
   233|
   234|Clarity Issue:
   235|"Many teams don't have a systematic approach" is vague. What's missing?
   236|Is it: lack of time? lack of structure? lack of tools? lack of accountability?
   237|Be specific: "Many teams conduct discovery ad-hoc, without rhythm or documentation."
   238|
   239|Evidence Gap:
   240|"A good discovery process should include interviews, surveys, and data analysis" —
   241|This feels like a template. Does YOUR process include all three, or do you recommend adapting?
   242|Better: "Our most effective process combines lightweight interviews (10-15 people) with
   243|behavioral data analysis. Surveys are optional, depending on what you're learning."
   244|
   245|Specificity Win:
   246|"Monthly" is too vague. Better: "Every two weeks, or whenever you're making a decision that
   247|affects your roadmap" (shows it's driven by needs, not calendar)
   248|
   249|Specific Line Edit:
   250|Original: "You should also document what you learn."
   251|Suggested: "Document the insight (not the transcript). Three bullet points max: What surprised you?
   252|What changed your thinking? What's the next question?"
   253|Why: Specific enough for readers to actually do it
   254|
   255|Ready to revise this section?
   256|```
   257|
   258|### Writer's Revision
   259|
   260|> "Product managers often struggle with discovery because they're doing it ad-hoc, without rhythm or documentation. Every decision you make should be informed by recent customer learning. Here's the process I've used at [Company A] and [Company B]: lightweight interviews (10-15 people) + behavioral data analysis. Every two weeks, or whenever you're making a major decision, document the insight—just three bullets: What surprised you? What changed your thinking? What's the next question?"
   261|
   262|**My Response:** "Better. You've added specificity, examples, and your voice. The framework is now actionable. Ready for section 3?"
   263|
   264|---
   265|
   266|## Pattern Recognition Across Examples
   267|
   268|What makes these work:
   269|
   270|1. **Clear outline before drafting** — Not all-at-once writing; structured thinking first
   271|2. **Section-by-section feedback** — Early feedback prevents downstream mistakes
   272|3. **Writer's voice throughout** — I suggest, they choose; final piece reads like them
   273|4. **Specific, actionable feedback** — Not "good work" but "here's what works and why"
   274|5. **Research serves the voice** — Sources amplify authority, don't override perspective
   275|6. **Respect for deadlines** — Quick iteration, not perfectionism
   276|7. **Learning as you write** — Patterns from section 1 → applied independently in section 3
   277|
   278|All of these pieces work together to create writing that's both excellent and authentically theirs.
   279|
